summary: Atwood argues that most software users are 'perpetual intermediates' who quickly graduate from beginner status but never become experts, and that this intermediate majority should be the primary design target. He cites Alan Cooper's observation that beginners rapidly move to intermediate status or abandon the product entirely, while experts are rare and transient. Rather than designing for the extremes, Atwood advocates for the Inductive User Interface (IUI) approach, which combines web conventions like back buttons and activity-centric pages with traditional GUI richness. He points to Microsoft Money 2000 as the exemplar of this approach and urges developers to adopt these patterns immediately.
key_points: Most users are perpetual intermediates at nearly everything they do, including software - expertise is rare and narrow
Beginners quickly become intermediates or quit entirely; nobody willingly stays incompetent
Software development is traditionally pulled between developer advocates (experts) and marketing (beginners), leaving intermediates underserved
The Inductive User Interface combines web UI metaphors (back button, hyperlinks, single-page activities) with rich desktop capabilities
Stealing successful UI patterns from the web is 'such an utter no-brainer' that the only question is why adoption is so slow
money_quotes: I am a perpetual intermediate at a vast array of tasks, and expert at only a very, very tiny number of tasks.
When people achieve an adequate level of experience and ability, they generally stay there forever.
I think intermediate users are the only users that matter.
Beginners should either become intermediates or, in a manner of speaking, die trying.
The difficulty of maintaining a high level of expertise means that experts come and go rapidly.
Never write what you can steal.
Stealing the wildly successful web UI metaphors is such an utter no-brainer.
key_insight: Design software for the permanent majority of intermediate users, not for the transient extremes of beginners and experts.
